text: HIGH COURT ey fea (HIGH COURT OF AUSTRALIA) BEETHAM PLAINTIFF, TREMEARNE . DEFENDANT, ON APPEAL FROM THE SUPREME COURT OF QUEENSLAND. H.C or A, Local authority—By-law1905. travel—Costs.

Unreasonableness—Tick pest—Notice to dip—Pe Respondent was prosecuted under a by-law made under the Local d a Act 1902 (Queensland). By clause 2 of the by-law, it was provided that's pase, 5 owner of stock shall commence to travel the same into, out of, or thro shire unless he has obtained from an inspector a permit authori do." Before commencing to travel sto to the inspector seven days' notice in writing of his intention, received notice from the inspector, given under another clause of the requiring him to dip certain of his cattle within seven days, He thera without any permit, but intending to comply with the notice, took te« 'out of the shire to a dip in another shire. Held, that the notice to dip did not amount to a permit to travel out ft shire, and that the respondent having taken the cattle out of the shite permit was guilty of an offence under the by-law.

A complaint was laid under By-law No. 7* of the Shire | *By-law No. 7, clause 2:—"No out of, or through the Shire ge owner of stock shall commence to travel days' notice in writing to the same into, out of, or through the of his intention s0 todo, Shire unless he has obtained from an _On receipt of suchnotioe, Inspector a permit authorizing him so tor may issue to such owaet to do. to travel such stock into, Every owner of stock shall, before through the Shire as the cut commencing to travel the same into, ot if in the opinion of OLR} OF AUSTRALIA. «gSouthport by appellant against respondent, that he, "being the twner of stock, travelled the said stock ont of the Shire of Southwithout obtaining from the Inspector a permit authorizing "fim to do so as provided by clause 2, By-law 7." 'The by-law rrasmade under the Local Authorities Act 1902, 'The magistrate "jaring dismissed the complaint, stated the following ease for the opinion of the Supreme Court :— | The appellant, an Inspector under the by-law, eaused the respon"dent to be served with the following notice :— Dear Sir, I have "jnxpected your cattle in the paddock opposite the Sugar Mill, "and find them badly infested with ticks.

If you do not dip them within seven days, I shall take action at once." 'The respondent after the receipt of the notice and in consequence thereof caused the cattle to be taken out of the Shire to a dip known as " «Stephen's Dip," and there duly and properly dipped the cattle. 'he respondent bond fide believed that he was complying with "the notice and that the said notice amounted to a permit to take the eattle out of the Shire to the dip. There was a dip at hich the stock could have been dipped within the boundaries of the Shire, but Stephen's dip was the nearest and most convenient, The question of law for the decision of the Supreme Court was whether the magistrate was right in dismissing the complaint, The Full Court (Cooper C.J. and Power J.) dismissed the appeal with costs, Fees, (with him Stumm) for respondent, moved to rescind the onler for special leave to appeal, on an affidavit that a resolution was passed by the Southport Shire Council that appellant's 'dicitors be instructed to discontinue the proceedings. Other affidavits on the point were read. _ Lukin, for appellant. 'There has been no withdrawal of our 'structions to proceed.

HIGH COURT * Per Curiam —Leave to appeal was granted on the apy of a duly authorized officer of the Council. 'The appeal is still prosecuted in the name of that officer. It is alleged that k authority has since been withdrawn, but we think that the fat not established. Lukin, for appellant.

The Fuli Court judgment proce mainly on the construction of the word "through" in theby Here the cattle were taken "out of" the Shire. 'The effetaf decision is that immediately notice to dip is given the eattlen be taken out of the Shire. Under those circumstances, theby. would be useless. [Barron J.—Seven days' notice is required when a pemit is asked to travel stock out of the Shire. The notice to dip seven days' notice.

Before obtaining the permit to trav, th time for dipping might have expired.] If it were thought proper to dip within the Shire, the pemit to go outside for the purpose of dipping would be refused. Where a by-law is capable of two possible constructions, construction should be given which will support it and not that which will render it void: Curtis v. Stovin (1); and although one portion of a by-law may be void, the remaining portion m be enforceable: Dyson v.

London and North Western Railway Company (2); Metropolitan Transit Commissioners v. Bam (3). [Barton J, referred to Slattery v. Naylor (4)J Where a by-law is within the scope of the legislative powers a local authority, the Court will be slow to avoid it on th ground of unreasonableness: Browne v.
Cowley (5); Sankey | Plover (6); Calder v. Lewis (7). Mens rea is not an essen ingredient in offences of this kind : Zhe Queen v.
Prince (8). Feez (with him Stumm), for respondent. No question of | reasonableness of the by-law was ever raised, nor is it (1) 22.Q.B.D. (2) 7Q.8.D, (8) 9Q.L. (4) 13 App.
C (5) 6QLJ., 234 dia 6) St. Re a 6. i (Po 150, per Grifth Oy p. 100. OB da LJ, B.C 12 "S02 OF AUSTRALIA. "gow.

The by-law placed the respondent in a dilemma, unless the rier to dip is construed to amount to a permit to travel the stock othedip. 'The appellant contends that the respondent committed snoffence against the by-law by moving without a permit. 'This je could not obtain till after seven days—the period within which jewas ordered to dip. If the Court was wrong, it was on the ~ question of the construction of the by-law ; it did not decide on the ground that the by-law was unreasonable. 'The Conrt held that the order to dip was in effect a permit to travel. 'The mere taking of stock to a dip is not a "travelling" of stock within the meaning of the by-law. [He cited Heaslop v. Burton (1)] 'The Queensland Criminal Code provides (sec. 24) that "a-person who does or omits to do an act under an honest and reasonable hut mistaken belief in the existence of any state of things is not criminally responsible for the act or omission to any greater extent than if the real state of things had been such as he believed to sist, The operation of this rule may be excluded by the express or implied provisions of the law relating to the subject." Here the respondent believed he was ordered to go to the dip, and that such order amounted to a permit to travel: Sherras v. de Rutzen @) _— [Grtrrrrit C..—That was not a mistake of fact, but as to the efitet of the document.] Whatever the decision of the Court may be, respondent could sily have been guilty of a mere technical offence, and should not be deprived of his costs: Forget v.

Ostigny (3). Lakin in veply. Cur. adv. vult. __ GhrrrrH C.J. 'This is an appeal from the Supreme Court of Queensland dismissing an appeal from justices who had refused : to convict the respondent upon a charge of a breach of a by-law, thecharge being that he had travelled stock out of the Shire of Southport without having obtained a permit authorizing him to os, 'The by-law was made by the Couneil of the Shire under M) (192) St.

H.C. or A. 1905, sae Beeruan v 'TreMeanne. Gritith C.. HIGH COURT the authority of the Local Government Act 1902, the end of the nineties what was called the tick pest had very serious thing in Queensland, and provision was made by legislature in the year 1898 by which large powers were 9 ferred upon the Diseases of Stock Board for preventin areas, and the moving of cattle from one area to any other an was prohibited under a heavy penalty.

In 1902 the legi conferred powers to deal with this pest upon local anth authorizing them to exereise those powers by by-laws. A the subjects on which they were authorized to make by: was "Regulating or prohibiting the introduction into or removal from the area or part thereof of any pest" (No. 41 they are empowered to make regulations " Preseribing methol and appliances for the effectual destruction of pests" term pest is defined by the Act as meaning "any animal orbin infesting or devouring any tree, plant, vegetable, or product thereof," and it includes "any insect matter or thing infesting causing disease in any animal which in each ease has be declared a pest under the Act." The pest dealt with by The second clause of the by-law provides that no owner of stick shall commence to travel the same into, out of, or through t Shire unless he has obtained from the Inspector a permit a izing him to do so. It goes on to provide that every 0 of stock shall before commencing to travel into, out oh through the Shire give seven days' notice of his intention s0 tod On receipt of the notice the Inspector may issue a permit to t the stock into, out of, or through the Shire as the ease may be if in the opinion of the Inspeetor the stock are affected by thep he may refuse to issue a permit unless and until they are dip in a dip as prescribed, or dressed or treated with certain press medicaments, Now, in construing the by-law regard m OLR] OF AUSTRALIA. jal to the nature of the evil intended to be dealt with.

These ticks are small insects, infesting animals sometimes in very large umbers,and when stock travel the insects are apt to drop off, and nay s0 infect the whole of the neighbourhood through which the animals pass. Danger, therefore, is incurred by moving stock. 'There are other dangers, for the inseets can be carried by birds, but that cannot be avoided. 'The danger the by-law seeks to avoid is the transmission of the pest by travelling animals, and the legislature authorized the local authority not only to protect the Shire jtslf, but to protect other parts of the State, for they are authorized to prohibit the removal of stock from the Shire as yellas its introduction. Stopping at clause 2 for a moment, there jsno doubt as to the meaning of the words "no owner shall commence to travel stock out of the Shire without a permit." That is a positive prohibition.

If the owner of stock desires to take his stock out of the Shire he must give notice to the Inspector, "aul ask for a permit, but the Inspector is not bound to grant it. On inspection he may think that the stock are in such a condition that to move them would impose a danger of infection upon the stock in the paddocks or in the country through which thy would pass to get out of the Shire. He may, therefore, in his discretion, for the protection of other stock in the Shire itself, aid for the protection of stock out of the Shire, require that, Iefore they are moved, they shall be dipped or treated so as toavoid that danger.

And it is quite clear that the intention of -theowner cannot make any difference. The danger is just as grat when the intention is to go to the nearest dip as it is when it is to take the stock to the slaughter yard. Clause Sof the By-laws provides: "When any stock are, in the opinion ofan Inspector, affected by the pest he may, by notice in writing signed by him and given to or served on the owner of such stock, order that, before the expiration of seven days from the dhy on which such notice is given to or served on such owner," the stock shall be dipped or treated in the prescribed manner.

If the order is to dip them, the owner has three courses open. He may dip them on his own premises if he can. If he has not a dip this own premises, he may propose to dip them somewhere else, "tul the other place may be within the Shire, or outside of the H.C. oF A. 1905. — Beernan Tremearne, Gritien © 3, 'HL ©. oF A. 1905, — BrerHa e. 'TREMEARNE.
Gritith C9. HIGH COURT Shire. If he desives to take them out of the Shire, the Hrovi of clause 2 of the by-laws applies, which says that he mera a permit before he does so.

All that seems perfectly plain; th is no ineonsisteney between the two provisions, Tpass now to the facts of the case. Tt is admitted that respondent had received a notice from the Inspector of the requiring him to dip his cattle. He thereupon proceeded to them out of the Shire without asking for a permit, and thereyp he was charged with a breach of the by-law. Obviously be hi transgressed the by-law.

The defence set up was that the to dip the stock amounted of itself to a permit to take them of the Shire. The reasoning upon which that contention is h isnot very clear, but it was put in this way: 'The notice to dip a notice to do so within seven days ; and, as an owner desiring t take stock out of the Shire must give seven days' notice before he does it, the two periods conflict with one another. He is requinel to get them dipped within seven days; he proposes to take out of the Shire to get them dipped; and he must give seven days notice of his intention to take them out: therefore it would le unreasonable under these circumstances to require him to get permit before taking them out of the Shire.

Why? He need at take them out of the Shire unless he likes. If he prefers to take them out he must obey the law, that is to say, he must not expose his neighbours to the danger of infection without getting a permit, which will only be granted after proper precautions to avoid danger of infection to the intervening roads.

The learned (hi Justice, who delivered the judgment of the Supreme Court, spoke of the two clauses as incompatible. I have endeavoured to show that they are not. He said it would be unreasonable to expel a compliance with clause 2, which says that the owner must not take stock out of the Shire without a permit, from an owner who had received a notice under clause 5.

If the order to dip stock could be construed as operating in itself as an order requiritg the owner to take them out of the Shire for the purpose posit the absolute prohibition contained in by-law No. 2 might # applied to such a case, be unreasonable. But the one T have shown, be so construed. On the plain construction by-law 2 the defendant has committed a breach of it, and thee OLR] OF AUSTRALIA. 'snothing unreasonable in reading it as a positive prohibition inst removing stock without a permit.

That the enforcement ofthe by-law under particular circumstances might be considered Jord does not make the by-law itself unreasonable. We have othing to'do with the harshness of the proceeding. We allowed "thisappeal to be brought, because the question whether clause 2 construed in such a case as an absolute prohibition—as it is in plain terms—was reasonable seemed to be a matter of considershe public importance.

I cannot therefore see my way to any "ther conclusion than that the defendant committed a breach of syalid by-law for which he should have been convicted. Some "fiffeulty appears to have been created in the minds of the learned Judges from the use of the word "through" in clause 2 which "ays: "No owner of stock shall commence to travel the same, fnio, out of, or through the Shire." It was contended that the word "through" refers only to stock passing through the Shire fromend to end, in at one boundary and out at another. In answer tothat it was said that, as both going into and going out of the Shire are expressly prohibited, the word if so construed would be "unnecessary and meaningless.

And it was pointed out that there may be as much danger in stock travelling from one end of the Shite to the other, although all the time within its boundaries, as there would be in taking them out of it. But these are matters vith which we are not concerned, We are dealing only with the lplaw so far as it prohibits the taking of stock out of the Shire. Texpress no opinion as to the meaning of the word "through," nor do I express any dissent from the opinion of the learned Judges as to its meaning, which is certainly the most convenient tnd beneficial construction to be put upon it.

It will be time enough to deal with that when the question arises before us, but pon the meaning of the provision that an owner shall not com_ limes totravel stock out of the Shire, without a permit, I eannot 'tertain any doubt. For these reasons I think that the appeal ftom the justices ought to have been allowed; but under the leumstances of the case, as it appeared that the respondent bind fide believed that, he was authorized in doing what he did, I think the appeal might very properly have been allowed without "st. In my opinion this appeal should be allowed with costs, HC. oF A. 1905, ~ Brernam 'Trewease, Grimitn C3.
H.O. oF A. 1905. Beernan 'TREMKARNI Grifith C2. HIGH COURT and the appeal to the Supreme Court should have been but without costs, and that that order should be substitut the order made. : Barton J.

Iam entirely of the same opinion, and J there is no necessity to add anything. OCoxxor J. The defence was put upon two grounds by Feez, The first was that the document, which is called a note really amounted to a permit enabling the stock to be travelled of the Shire.

The words of the notice on the face of them cann bear that interpretation. There are no words of permit. It a notice that if the stock are not dipped within seven days, Inspector, the Chairman, will take action at once, It is said th the notice ought to be interpreted as a permit, because it is u reasonable that any further permit should be required under circumstances.

Now, if one looks at the by-laws it seems pl that a permit is required before stock are allowed to travel, ifth regulations are to be interpreted so as to effect the object which th whole of these regulations aim at. When once stock have adjudged by the Inspector to be infected, these regulations pt stock in a certain sense under the control of the law. The Inspec him, The first of these duties is that he himself has to see, unl regulation 5, that the stock are dipped.

The words of regulation are: "Such dipping, dressing, or treatment shall present while the dipping is going on. Now it is to enable that duty to be carried out—it is absolutely essen —that the dipping should take place within his juristietion The regulations give him no jurisdiction outside his own and to say that this notice to dip is a permit to take th stock out of the Shire would imply that he has given @ peut 0 take those stock somewhere out of his jurisdiction, to be dipps at a place in which he could not insist that the dipping should carried out under his superintendence. It appears tometh impossible on the words of this notice and in the ¢i OLR] OF AUSTRALIA. {have arisen, to interpret this notice as a permit.

It is juiced very reasonable that a permit should be required in sidition toa notice of this kind. What took place in this case rasan indication of that necessity. 'The stock were not travelled wy far. 'They were travelled something like three miles from the place in which they were inspected and declared infected. Some of them were taken over the river in a publie punt, and come of them travelled in a cart, and one can easily understand tat it might be a matter of very great importance that the Inspector should have some knowledge of the route that these infected stock were to take to the dip, or the dip to which they were Jetaken. Now the law contemplates that this permit shall be given inevery case as soon as cattle are found infected, and before they ean be moved. 'That enables the Inspector to have some con"tmlover where the cattle are to be taken.

It may be, in the "interests of the stock in the district, altogether a wrong thing to "take them to a particular dip. It may be convenient in the publie "interest to take them to some other dip, or it may be found impossile to take them to any dip, and then the provision for treating "dock on the spot will be carried out. It appears to me that the -entrol of these matters must be in the hands of the Inspector, "thich would be impossible if his notice to dip cattle which he lus found to be infected is to be interpreted "them to be dipped wherever the owner thinks fit. If it is a per"nit to take stock to this dipping place, then equally it would be permit to take them 50 miles away into the next Shire, if that Inppened to be the nearest dipping place.

An argument has also len urged by Mr. Feez, which certainly impressed me at first, "ind that was that you could not interpret the word "travel" in Se 2of the by-law so as to apply to a case of this kind. As is well known the word "travel" in connection with stock has ferent meanings in the different States. 'The word "travel" 'nmany of the Stock Acts, and I think in some of those in this State, has a special meaning, but under these by-laws and in Connection with this subject "travel" has no special meaning.

Therefore we must interpret the word in its ordinary sense, 'ving regard to the objects of the Statute. It is suid that if we 'ulerpret the word "travel" in the sense applicable to the eireuma permit to take H.C. or A. 1905, — Beevuas 'TreMEaRNE, OConnor J. H.C. or A. 1905.

Brernawt 'TREMEARSE. 'Connor J. HIGH COURT stances here, that is to say, travelling toa dip after notes has) given, then we are putting the owner of stock in the poi having to comply with the impossible, but T donot think tht so. What is the first step which the owner must take aeeonting these by-laws after he has had notice served upon him? 'Thef step isto geta permit.

That permit need not be delayed fors days. It probably would be given at once, because the eaitis un not move until the permit is given. As soon as that pei given the cattle may be moved. But it is said that the seed of the by-law would come into operation—that before commentgp to travel the stock the owner must give seven days' notice to Inspector of his intention so to do.

I think it is very cleat under these cireumstances that regulation would not be appliahl, and if it could be applied, the Inspector, who himself had adjua the stock to be infected, and who himself had given the peri could not be permitted in any Court to say that there wasab of the law in the owner not having given seven days' note writing to the Inspector of his intention to travel. I can se no inconsistency, and I see nothing unreasonable in the work ing of these regulations as to dipping. I think, therefor, th the word "travel" must be used in a sense which will give eft to the Act.

The same considerations apply on that point, as on the other point I dealt with, that is to say, stock when one they have been adjudged to be infected, must be dipped unil the control of the Inspector, and thus the interpretation of the lav which gives the word " travel" its ordinary meaning, that istosy, the moving cattle from the place where they are insp anywhere, in any direction, is that which best carries out th intention of the Act. It is not necessary to decide wl moving cattle from one property to another belonging to thesim owner would be travelling them. A question of that sort ms arise, and it may cause some difficulty in the interpretation of th word, but in this case there is no doubt whatever that there was'® travelling," because the stock were removed from the place where they were inspected to a place out of the Shire, and they! never returned.

For these reasons I entirely coneur in the joie ment of His Honor the Chief Justice on the ease generally and as to the costs. OF AUSTRALIA, 593 Appeal allowed. Order appealed from disH.C. or A. charged, case remitted to justices with 195 directions to convict. The respondent to pomay pay the costs of this appeal.

The Constitution (63 : 64 Viet., c. 12), secs. 71, 73, 1903 (Vo. 6 of 1903), secs. 30, 38, 34, 39, 63, 7 (No.1105), se. 127—A uthority of Parliament to confer appellate federal juriedi tion on other Court than High Court—Whether appellate jurisdiction conferred om State Courts— Offence against Commonwealth Law—Summary Conviction— Appeal to State Court—Remedy where Court denies jurisdiction— Mandamus gyipth 63, and Appeal, Barton d. 5, 16, 77—The Judiciary Act H.C. or A, 80—Juatices Act 1890( Victoria) 1905. j Mexnovn August 2, 3, 7. Where a Court of a State declines jurisdiction in a matter as to which it is invested with federal jurisdiction, the remedy is by recourse to the appellate Jutisdiction of the High Court. 'The federal jurisdiction which Parliament is by sec. 77 of the Constitution suthorized to confer upon the Courts of the several States, and upon federal Gourts other than the High Court, includes both original and appellate Jotisdiction,
